icd_category: D51.0 -- Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia due to intrinsic factor deficiency
one_liner: A 68-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive fatigue, a sore red tongue, and new tingling in her feet with unsteady gait.

stage2.hpi_extension: Her fatigue has been accompanied by symmetric paresthesias in the toes and fingers that have progressed proximally, as well as difficulty walking in the dark because she cannot feel her feet well. She has mild memory fogginess. Her tongue has become smooth, red, and painful, making spicy foods intolerable. She denies overt GI bleeding, weight loss, or diarrhea. She has not taken any multivitamins. She has type 1 diabetes and autoimmune hypothyroidism.
stage2.pmh: Type 1 diabetes mellitus, Hashimoto hypothyroidism, vitiligo
stage2.family_history: Sister with pernicious anemia; mother with Graves disease
stage2.social_history: Retired librarian, non-smoker, rare alcohol, eats meat and dairy regularly
stage2.physical_exam: General: pale, icteric sclerae (mild). HEENT: smooth, beefy-red tongue (atrophic glossitis), angular cheilitis. Cardiovascular: regular with soft flow murmur. Abdomen: soft, no hepatosplenomegaly. Neuro: decreased vibration and proprioception in the lower extremities bilaterally (dorsal column involvement), positive Romberg sign, mild spasticity and hyperreflexia at the knees with upgoing toes (lateral corticospinal tract involvement), wide-based ataxic gait. Mental status: slow recall. No cranial nerve deficits.

lab_results.result: Hgb 9.1 g/dL (low), MCV 118 fL (markedly elevated, macrocytic), WBC 4.2, platelets 130. Reticulocyte count low. Smear: oval macrocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils with 6+ lobes, anisopoikilocytosis. LDH elevated, indirect bilirubin mildly elevated (ineffective erythropoiesis).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Macrocytic megaloblastic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils is the pathognomonic smear finding of B12 (or folate) deficiency. Mild intramedullary hemolysis from ineffective erythropoiesis explains the elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin.

lab_results.result: Serum vitamin B12: 98 pg/mL (markedly low). Serum folate: normal. Methylmalonic acid: markedly ELEVATED. Homocysteine: markedly ELEVATED.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated methylmalonic acid AND homocysteine confirm B12 deficiency and distinguish it from folate deficiency (in which only homocysteine rises). Both reflect the two B12-dependent reactions: methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA (requires B12) and homocysteine to methionine (requires B12 and folate).

lab_results.result: Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies: POSITIVE. Anti-parietal cell antibodies: positive. Schilling test not performed.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies confirm pernicious anemia, the autoimmune form of B12 deficiency. Autoantibodies destroy gastric parietal cells, eliminating intrinsic factor and preventing ileal B12 absorption.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on intramuscular cyanocobalamin. Within weeks her tongue heals and her hemoglobin rises; however, her lower extremity paresthesias only partially improve, reflecting some irreversible damage to the dorsal columns.

debrief.classic_presentation: Vitamin B12 deficiency classically presents in older patients with macrocytic anemia, glossitis, and neurologic symptoms. The hallmark neurologic syndrome is subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord, which affects the dorsal columns (loss of vibration and position sense, Romberg sign) and the lateral corticospinal tracts (spasticity, hyperreflexia, upgoing toes). Etiologies include pernicious anemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells and loss of intrinsic factor), strict veganism, terminal ileal disease or resection (Crohn disease, surgery), bacterial overgrowth, Diphyllobothrium latum infection, and chronic proton pump inhibitor/metformin use.
debrief.pathophysiology: Dietary B12 is bound by R-factor in the stomach, released by pancreatic proteases in the duodenum, then bound by intrinsic factor (secreted by gastric parietal cells) for absorption in the terminal ileum. B12 is a cofactor for methionine synthase (homocysteine to methionine, which remethylates and regenerates tetrahydrofolate) and for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA). Impairment of the first reaction causes megaloblastic anemia; impairment of the second leads to accumulation of methylmalonyl-CoA, which is incorporated into abnormal myelin, producing the neurologic findings.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treat with intramuscular cyanocobalamin (or high-dose oral B12 in some cases). Hematologic parameters improve within days to weeks; however, long-standing neurologic damage may be only partially reversible. ALWAYS check B12 before giving folate alone in a macrocytic anemia -- folate corrects the anemia but fails to correct the neurologic disease and can precipitate or worsen subacute combined degeneration by depleting available methyl groups.
high_yield_pearls: B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration: dorsal columns (proprioception/vibration) PLUS lateral corticospinal tracts (upper motor neuron signs).
Pernicious anemia = autoantibodies to intrinsic factor; associated with other autoimmune disease (type 1 DM, Hashimoto, vitiligo).
Elevated methylmalonic acid AND homocysteine = B12 deficiency. Elevated homocysteine ALONE with normal MMA = folate deficiency.
Hypersegmented neutrophils (>=5 lobes) on smear plus oval macrocytes = megaloblastic anemia.
Never give folate alone for a macrocytic anemia without checking B12 -- it can unmask or worsen subacute combined degeneration.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) -- Hematology and Oncology chapter, 'Macrocytic anemias / Megaloblastic anemia -- Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency' section.
